features.description: The feature detects the word "float" or "floating" in code contexts, particularly in variable types, class names, or API parameters.
This feature detects last names in author/maintainer metadata, particularly in Python package setup/config files (e.g., "Cox", "Combs").
The feature detects instances of web-related terms like "webhook," "Browser," or "Web" in code contexts, particularly in Python imports, class definitions, or method calls.
This feature detects tokens that are part of domain names, URLs, or code identifiers, often following dots or slashes in technical contexts.

This feature detects Django model field class definitions, specifically focusing on the syntactic pattern where a field type (e.g., `Field`, `AutoField`) appears within a model class inheritance or declaration.
This feature detects the Python `optparse` module import, specifically when the word "parse" appears in the import statement (e.g., `from optparse import...`).
The feature detects Unix-style hidden directory paths in configuration files, specifically the `~/.` prefix before directory or file names.
This feature detects the term "relational" (and variants) in contexts related to databases, data frameworks, or object-relational mapping.
The feature detects the `handle` method definition in Django management command classes, specifically the `(self` token in the method signature.
Feature detects slice operations (`[:]`) and unpacking operators (`*`, `**`) in Python code, particularly in string formatting and array indexing.
The feature detects variations of the word "determine" and its derivatives in code contexts related to decision-making or configuration.
This feature detects digits within long numeric strings, often in IDs, phone numbers, or addresses, particularly when they're part of structured data formats.
The feature detects the phrase "entry point" in Python docstrings or comments, specifically marking the main execution start of a script or module.
This feature detects Python decorators using `@pytest.mark` in test functions, specifically marking test cases with pytest's custom markers.
The feature detects the substring "shed" in Python import statements, particularly in module names like "toolshed" or "ToolShed".
Feature detects Python import statements in Django migration files, specifically `from django.db import ...` patterns.
This feature detects numeric arguments in `time.sleep()` calls, specifically representing sleep durations in seconds.
The feature detects instances of "DiffieHellman" or its variants (e.g., "DH", "Hellman") in cryptographic key exchange code contexts.
The feature represents the syntactic role of `.apps` in Python import statements specifically from the Django framework.
Feature detects variable assignments where "VERSION" is assigned a version number or tuple.
The feature detects instances of template `loader` objects in Python code, particularly in Django and Jinja2 template systems.
The feature detects instances of "gateway" in code, specifically in API, payment, network, and backend contexts, often as a variable or class name.
This feature detects Django migration file templates, specifically the line before the `class Migration(migrations.Migration):` declaration, often involving imports and dependencies.
The feature detects the default port number "5000" in Python web server configurations, typically in Flask or similar frameworks.
This feature detects the substring "angled" in URLs or package names related to the "Tangled" web framework.
This feature detects version suffix tags in Python packages (e.g., -alpha, beta, -dev, rc) indicating pre-release or development stages.
This feature detects Python `import` statements specifically for the Flask framework, typically in the form `from flask import [something]`.
The feature detects forward slashes in GitHub repository URLs, specifically in the path segment between the domain and the repository name.
The feature detects the substring "th" or "fo" within code comments or string literals, often indicating abbreviations or placeholders.
The feature detects the token "panels" in Python/Django debug toolbar configuration strings.
Feature detects the token "mb" in Alembic revision identifier comments within Python docstrings.
The feature detects and highlights digits within IPv4 addresses, specifically focusing on the third octet of the IP.
The feature detects instances of error handling for connection-related exceptions (e.g., `ConnectionError`, `ClientError`) in Python code, particularly with `requests` and `boto3` libraries.
Feature detects tokens in all-caps or mixed-case identifiers, often constants or configuration variables (e.g., `_DEFAULT`, `IS_ITACLOUD`, `Factory`).
Feature detects technical terms related to "Information Analysis," "Spatial Analysis," or "Systems Analysis" in software/metadata contexts.
This feature detects tokens that are part of variable names or identifiers in code, particularly those following underscores or in all-caps contexts.
The feature detects the Python package name "praw" in import statements and setup.py dependencies.
The feature detects HTTP status codes in the 400 range (client errors), particularly focusing on codes like 400, 401, 404, and 405.
The feature detects decimal points in numeric literals or string representations within code contexts.
Detects dictionary key checks using the `.keys()` method in Python.
This feature detects numeric digits (particularly '0') in IP addresses, file paths, or numeric identifiers within technical/code contexts.
The feature detects mentions of prime numbers in code, including function names, variables, and comments related to primality testing or prime generation.
This feature detects instances of the word "capacity" (or variants like "_capacity") in code contexts, particularly as a variable, attribute, or parameter name.
This feature detects the syntactic role of `tuple` as a constructor function converting iterables or arguments into tuple objects.
This feature detects the opening double square bracket `[[`, commonly used in wikis, templates, or nested data structures.
Closing punctuation (commas or parentheses) in docstrings or comments indicating the end of parenthetical or explanatory phrases.
This feature detects instances of `sphinx.ext` in Python configuration files, specifically within `extensions` lists for Sphinx documentation setups.
The feature detects the Python library name "twisted" in import statements or code context.
The feature detects the substring "cloud" in Python imports or the letter "t" in "washingtontimes" email/URL contexts.
This feature detects the word "Simple" (or variations like "A") in package descriptions, particularly in Python `setup()` configurations, highlighting minimalist or easy-to-use tool claims.
This feature detects the word "or" in GNU license boilerplate text, specifically in version selection clauses like "version X or any later version."
The feature detects Python import statements involving the modules "sahara" or "st2common" as the imported package name.
This feature detects Tkinter window management methods, specifically `winfo_*` and `wm_*` calls, used for querying or modifying window properties.
The feature detects exclamation marks (`!`) in code contexts, particularly in YAML tags, regex patterns, and special character strings.
The feature detects the token `_VERSION` in variable names, specifically in Python package version assignments like `PACKAGE_VERSION = 'x.y.z'`.
The feature detects digits in Django migration filenames within dependency lists, specifically in auto-generated timestamp portions (e.g., `0014_auto_20170129_2236`).
This feature detects the token 'ipython' in Python package dependency lists, specifically within 'extras_require' or 'install_requires' sections.
The feature detects the word "We" in code comments, particularly when it introduces explanations or justifications for implementation decisions.
The feature detects the token `/lib` in paths within system library directories, often in configuration or build scripts.
Feature detects parameter type annotations, specifically the word "string" in docstrings or function parameter declarations.
The feature detects the substring "ino" in email addresses or names, particularly in "@compassion.ch" email signatures in code comments.
The feature detects the token "ur" in the substring "waldur" within Python import statements from the Waldur project.
This feature detects references to static file handling in web frameworks, particularly for serving assets, files, or server configurations.
The feature detects instances of `subprocess.CalledProcessError` in Python exception handling for failed subprocess calls.
Feature detects instances of "peek" in copyright attribution lines, specifically in Mark Peek's email address (<mark@peek.org>).
The feature detects the number "1" in lists or arrays, particularly when it appears in descending sequences or as part of consecutive element checks.
This feature detects the substring "ad" within the word "datadog" in Python import statements and code contexts related to Datadog integrations.
This feature detects the Python `import` statement specifically for the `lxml` library, focusing on its `html` and `etree` modules.
This feature detects tokens that are parts of compound identifiers or paths in code, often following a dot or underscore.
The feature detects closing quotes or commas in Python string literals or attribute names, often following '__name' or path segments.
This feature detects inline code comments or documentation strings (marked with `#` or `"""/'''`) that are empty or contain only whitespace.
This feature detects the syntactic pattern "://" within URL strings, specifically in Python code involving HTTP/HTTPS requests.
The feature detects the token "splunk" or variants in code contexts, particularly in Python imports, module paths, and Splunk-related environment variables.
This feature detects author names or attribution markers in code metadata (like `author=`, `__author__`, or email addresses), particularly focusing on Turkish names or usernames.
This feature detects SQL database schema migration operations, specifically the `alter_column` or `AlterField` methods modifying column properties.
This feature detects the `-O` or `-o` flag in command-line arguments for `wget` or `curl`, indicating file output redirection.
The feature detects the `name` parameter in Python `setup()` function calls within `distutils.core` import statements.
Middle initials in names or email addresses, often between first and last names or in usernames.
The feature detects instances of `boto3.client()` calls in Python code, specifically when initializing AWS service clients.
The feature detects Python imports from Django's `django.contrib` module, specifically focusing on the `.contrib` segment in import statements.
The feature detects mentions of "Heroku" in code contexts, particularly in configuration, deployment, and environment settings.
This feature detects the word "exception" in code contexts related to error handling, logging, or traceback formatting.
The feature detects comment headers in Python files, specifically lines starting with `#` that describe file purpose, authorship, or copyright.
This feature detects email addresses (specifically "gmail") in author attribution lines within code documentation or metadata.
The feature detects tokens representing dictionary keys or key-related operations in Python code, such as accessing, defining, or iterating over keys.
This feature detects the string "pk" (primary key) in variable names, URL patterns, and Django model/query contexts.
This feature detects the `key` parameter in Python's `sorted()` function, specifically when used with lambda functions or `operator.itemgetter` to define custom sorting criteria.
The feature detects the method call `.isoformat()` applied to datetime objects for converting them to ISO 8601 string format.
This feature detects method or attribute names (like `replace`, `fields`, `make`, `face`, `bot`) used in Python code contexts, often within object-oriented operations or special method calls.
The feature detects socket type constants (`.SOCK_STREAM` or `.SOCK_DGRAM`) in Python socket initialization code.
The feature detects the word "room" (case-sensitive) in class definitions, foreign key relations, and model/viewset references in Python/Django code.
This feature detects whitespace tokens separating consecutive class or function definitions in Python code.
This feature detects code related to setting cache expiration times or cache keys, particularly in Python using methods like `cache.set` or `memcache.set`.
The feature detects the dot in "2.0" within Apache License version number URLs.
This feature detects the port number "3306" in configuration files or database connection strings, typically for MySQL database setups.
This feature detects Python import statements where `os` is being imported, particularly in contexts involving file system operations or environment variables.
The feature detects the first enum value declaration in a Python Enum class, specifically the token after the opening brace.
Feature detects URLs containing "/t" in paths, often in Django ticket references or GitHub repository links.
This feature represents the syntactic role of the term "global" as a modifier or identifier in configurations, permissions, and system-wide settings.
The feature detects the "/licenses" path segment in Apache Software Foundation license URL patterns.
The feature detects the word "whether" in boolean parameter descriptions or return value documentation in code comments.
This feature detects the word "Lesser" in the context of GNU Lesser General Public License (LGPL) mentions in software licensing text.
The feature detects the word "google" in various contexts, particularly in email domains, URLs, and software dependencies.
This feature detects Python imports of the `hashlib` module, specifically when it appears at the start of a file or script.
This feature detects the left angle bracket `<` in URLs within markdown-style links, specifically in code documentation or license text.
The feature detects the number "59" in addresses or version strings, particularly in Free Software Foundation references and software versioning.
This feature detects newline characters (`\n`) used in Python print statements for formatting output, often in testing or logging contexts.
This feature detects Django model form imports, specifically `.models` in import paths and `ModelForm` class references.
Feature detects single-letter tokens following punctuation (commas, hyphens) in technical/license documentation.
This feature detects docstring parameter sections marked by `Parameters` followed by a line of dashes (`---------`).
This feature detects the word "Reserved" in copyright notices, specifically in the phrase "All Rights Reserved" within license headers.
Feature detects descending order syntax (`'-'`) in `.order_by()` method calls in Python/Django database queries.
The feature detects the SQL "CREATE" command when used to define new database tables or indices.
This feature detects variable names or abbreviations in code contexts, particularly those related to configuration, logging, or documentation.
This feature detects the `name` parameter in Django migration files' `AlterModelOptions` operations, specifically within model configuration contexts.
This feature detects instances of importing or using Tornado's `IOLoop` (often misspelled as "iolo⟦op⟧") or Flask's `abort` function (marked as "⟦abort⟧").
The feature detects the substring "gir" in Python import statements, particularly from the Girder library or related modules.
The feature detects the term "foreground" (or its abbreviation "fg") in code contexts related to UI/display settings, colors, or process execution modes.
This feature detects the minor version number in Django-generated file headers (e.g., "1.11.X" where X is the specific patch version).
This feature detects the `.query` method in SQLAlchemy ORM calls for database queries in Python code.
Feature detects pluralized nouns or suffixes ("ators", "ion", "on") in code comments, imports, or configuration strings.
The feature detects the infinitive marker "to" in phrases describing an object or action to be performed (e.g., "the object to compare").
The feature detects the "max_length" parameter in Django model CharField declarations.
This feature detects RGB color values or color-related syntax in code, particularly focusing on numeric components within parentheses or brackets (e.g., `(239,239,239)`, `'rgb(0,192,0)'`).
This feature detects tokens representing whitespace padding for alignment in structured code or configuration files.
This feature detects numeric constants assigned to uppercase variable names, typically representing configuration parameters or constants in code.
The feature detects the substring "clulab" in Java package paths, specifically within "org.clulab" namespace references in code contexts.
The feature detects the number "3" in AWS S3 URLs, specifically in the "s3" subdomain (e.g., "s3.amazonaws.com").
The feature detects the domain ".org" in PyPI package repository URLs within Python package configuration files.
The feature detects the word "real" (or variants like "_real") in code contexts, particularly in test names, documentation, and descriptive strings.
This feature detects a space token immediately following "import sys" in Python code, often marking the start of subsequent imports or code.
Feature detects the Python shebang line (`#!/usr/bin/python`) indicating script interpreter declaration.
This feature detects instances of the `Key` class from the `boto.s3` library, particularly in contexts involving S3 bucket operations and file/key management.
The feature detects the word "official" in Python package metadata descriptions, particularly in the context of declaring library authorship or status.
The feature detects the word "detected" (or variations like "Detected") in contexts related to error messages, warnings, or system status checks.
This feature detects the keyword "while" in loop constructs, particularly in contexts where it initiates an infinite or conditional loop in Python code.
This feature detects the word "the" in the phrase "compliance with the License" within legal/license boilerplate text.
This feature detects special characters (especially `&`, `%`, `.`) within regex patterns, URLs, or code delimiters.
Feature detects commas separating exception variables in Python's `except Exception, var:` syntax (old-style or Python 2.x exception handling).
This feature detects URLs pointing to raw content on GitHubusercontent.com, specifically in code contexts involving file imports, downloads, or resource fetching.
The feature detects column headers in data files or code, specifically matching variations of "header" (like "headers", "HEADERS", or "_HEADERS") in CSV, spreadsheet, or data processing contexts.
This feature detects the `option_list` variable in Python command classes, specifically when defining CLI options with `make_option`.
The feature detects tab characters (`\t`) used as column separators in formatted text or code, often in alignment contexts or data tables.
This feature detects tokens related to government or organizational terms, particularly "department," "parliament," and "procurement," often in code or administrative contexts.
This feature detects Python import statements, specifically the keyword "from" when used in "from X import Y" syntax.
This feature detects instances of `current_user` in Flask applications, specifically when accessing the authenticated user object from Flask-Login.
This feature detects the token "ent" within the `textwrap.dedent` function, indicating its role in dedenting multi-line string literals in Python code.
The feature detects Python import statements involving submodules (e.g., ".interfaces") or references to generic "data" variables in code contexts.
The feature detects the token "datastore" in code contexts related to Google Cloud Datastore or database configurations.
This feature detects variations of the word "attempt" (e.g., "attempt", "attempts", "attempted") in code contexts, particularly in retry loops or error handling.
This feature detects the string "nd" in variable names or code contexts, particularly in abbreviations like "ndir" (new directory) or "ndim" (number of dimensions).
This feature detects code-related instances of the prefix "pref" (or variations like "⟦pref⟧") in configuration settings, variable names, and function calls, particularly in Python and shell scripts.
The feature detects the keyword `keep` in function arguments, particularly in NumPy operations and file-copying commands, indicating parameter preservation settings.
This feature detects imports from the `IPython.core` module, specifically when `.core` is directly referenced in import statements.
The feature detects the "://" substring in URLs within Apache License boilerplate text.
This feature detects nationalities, languages, or country-related terms (e.g., Russian, Chinese, German, British) in code comments, metadata, or configuration files.
This feature detects tokens that are part of hyphenated compound words or abbreviations in code/documentation contexts (e.g., "-yaml", "_gwas", "CHANGES").
The feature detects the colon in Python docstring type annotations following `:type` declarations.
Feature detects digits in alphanumeric codes or IDs within test functions or documentation contexts.
This feature detects Python code that uses `gevent.monkey.patch_all()` to monkey-patch standard libraries for asynchronous behavior.
The feature detects substrings within event or organization names, particularly "tedx" and similar patterns like "pycon" or "mozill".
This feature detects Sublime Text configuration files with the naming pattern `*.sublime-settings`, typically used for plugin or editor settings.
This feature detects instances of "redistribute" and "CONTRIBUTORS" in software license text, particularly in GNU and MIT license boilerplate.
Feature detects tokens related to "uglify" or "line" in JavaScript/CSS minification and pipeline configuration contexts.
The feature detects log format strings, specifically patterns starting with `LOG_FORMAT` or `__date__`, commonly used in Python logging configurations.
The feature detects the digit "1" in version numbers or protocol identifiers, particularly in regex patterns or OpenFlow configurations.
This feature detects Python import statements, specifically focusing on the module name segment (like "vumi" or "responses") being imported.
The feature detects the Django model field option `on_delete=models.SET_NULL` or its variants, specifically firing on `_NULL` in this context.
The feature detects the token "ative" in "declarative_base" from SQLAlchemy's declarative ORM base class import.
This feature detects code-related suffixes or substrings in programming contexts, particularly in module names, function names, and identifiers.
This feature detects the string "err" (case-insensitive) in code contexts, particularly in error handling, logging, or configuration settings like `numpy.seterr`.
Feature detects `.title` property access in web browser testing contexts, often in assertions about page titles.
The feature detects the word "Cinder" in code contexts, specifically in Python imports, class names, and OpenStack-related configurations.
The feature detects the string "bin" in file paths, particularly in virtualenv activation scripts and pip installation commands.
The feature detects occurrences of the string `'last_login'` in code contexts, particularly in database fields, serializers, and user model attributes.
The feature detects the term "surface" in Python code, specifically in pygame imports and Surface class references.
This feature detects temporary variable declarations (often named `tmp`) used in intermediate computations or data storage within code snippets.
The feature detects substrings related to eigenvalues or eigenvectors in code contexts, particularly "eig", "igen", and "wig" fragments.
This feature detects the keyword `expect` used as a variable name in test assertions or expected output contexts.
This feature detects Python import statements for the `webob` or `werkzeug` libraries, specifically when importing modules or submodules (e.g., `webob.exc`, `werkzeug.wrappers`).
This feature detects instances of MySQL database configurations where the username is set to "root" (often indicating default or admin access).
The feature detects the Python `from __future__ import` statement, specifically the `import` keyword in future import declarations.
Feature detects HTTP status codes starting with "4" (client errors) in API response handling.
The feature detects the `universal_newlines=True` parameter in Python's `subprocess` calls for text output handling.
This feature detects instances of the word "record" (or variations like "Record") in code contexts, particularly in variable names, class definitions, and data processing loops.
The feature detects the token "build" in file/directory paths, build system commands, or Python package configurations.
The feature detects the number "1" when it represents a boundary in numeric ranges (e.g., [0,1] or [0,100]).
The feature detects Python import statements involving the word "gene" or its variants in module/package names.
This feature detects digits in URLs that represent months in blog post dates (e.g., "04" for April, "09" for September).
Opening parenthesis or bracket in Python function calls or list definitions, often following print statements or method calls.
This feature detects instances of the Python library "networkx" being imported or referenced in code, particularly focusing on the string "networkx" or its variations (e.g., "network⟦x⟧").
The feature detects the `Request` class import or instantiation from Python's `urllib.request` or `urllib2` modules.
The feature detects the substring "azen" within the name "Pierre Cazenave" in Python package metadata or author attribution contexts.
The feature detects verbs in present participle form ("-ing") following the word "Error" in error messages, typically indicating an action that failed.
The feature detects Python function attribute access, specifically `__name__`, used for logging, registration, or introspection.
The feature represents a wildcard pattern (`*.`) in Python package configuration files, specifically for specifying file types.
Feature detects Python exception handling patterns, specifically `APIException` imports and `raise_exception=True` validation in Django REST framework.
The feature detects tokens containing "kk", "kin", "@", or "ic", often in URLs, code snippets, or special character patterns.
The feature detects occurrences of "Program" in Windows file paths or "Software" in software development metadata tags.
The feature detects the token "Intended" in Python package metadata classifiers, specifically in "Intended Audience" descriptions.
